Conclusions The studies presented here demonstrate that an experimentally based structural parameterization of the energetics has the necessary accuracy and resolution for the structure-based prediction of binding affinities and for the implementation of molecular design algorithms.

Acknowledgment Supported by grants from the National Institutes of Health (RR04328 and GM51362).

[7] M o l e c u l a r C r o w d i n g : A n a l y s i s o f E f f e c t s o f H i g h Concentrations of Inert Cosolutes on Biochemical Equilibria and Rates in Terms of Volume Exclusion

By ALLEN P. MINTON Introduction During the 1960s and early 1970s, pioneering investigations of the thermodynamic and functional properties of proteins in polymer solutions implicated volume exclusion by polymer to protein as a significant effector of protein function in such media. 1-5 Substantial experimental and theoretical literature 6'7 has accumulated attesting to the generality and importance of excluded volume phenomena as major determinants of macromolecular structure and function in "crowded" solutions, i.e., solutions resembling physiological media in their high total macromolecular content. This article reviews thermodynamic and statistical-thermodynamic approaches to the quantification of the concept of excluded volume and the relationship between excluded volume and reaction equilibria and kinetics. The objective of such a review is twofold: (1) to present the reader with a readily accessible set of quantitative relationships that permit one to estimate the magnitude 1 m. G. Ogston and C. F. Phelps, Biochem. J. 78, 827 (1960). 2 T. C. Laurent, Biochem. J. 89, 253 (1963). 3 T, C. Laurent and A. G. Ogston, Biochem. J. 89, 249 (1963). 4 E. E d m o n d and A. G. Ogston, Biochem. J. 109, 569 (1968). 5 T, C. Laurent, Eur. J. Biochem. 21, 498 (1971). 6 S. B. Z i m m e r m a n and A. P. Minton, Annu. Rev. Biophys. Biomol. Struct. 22, 27 (1993). 7 A. P. Minton, Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 8, 65 (1997). T. L. Hill and Y.-D. Chen, Biopolymers 12, 1285 (1973).

of alterations in chemical equilibria arising from the introduction of high concentrations of so-called "inert" substances into a reaction medium and (2) to discuss the relative usefulness of various approaches to the quantitative analysis of data obtained from experimental measurements of such effects. With respect to a particular chemical reaction (the "test reaction"), a restricted set of solute species is defined as generalized reactants [i.e., reactants, transition state complex(es), and products]. All equilibrium and rate constants are defined in terms of the generalized reactant species only. All other solute species present in the medium are defined to be background solutes (or, equivalently, cosolutes) or considered to be part of the solvent.* Thermodynamic Preliminaries

Influence of Background Solutes on Reaction Equilibria 9 Consider the general reaction in solution X l X l q- x z X 2 q- . . . q- x r X r ~- Xr+lXr+l q- Xr+2Xr+2 -4- . . . q- Xr+pXr+ p

where X~ denotes the ith species of reactant or product, xi denotes the stoichiometric coefficient of that species, and r and p denote the total numbers of reactant and product species, respectively. A unit of reaction corresponds to the conversion of a stoichiometric number of moles of each reactant to a stoichiometric number of moles of each product. The thermodynamic equilibrium constant at constant temperature and pressure is given by r+p

1-1 i=l * Such a definition is subject to s o m e ambiguity when a reactant (say A) interacts with a cosolute (say B) in such a m a n n e r as to stabilize A. O n e can treat the stabilizing interaction as a binding event in the formal sense, necessitating the redefinition of B and one or more complexes of composition AIBj as additional reactant species. Alternatively, one m a y treat the interaction as a perturbation of the chemical potential of A by B, without postulating the presence of complexes of A and B. A l t h o u g h the two approaches m a y be shown to be thermodynamically equivalent, 8 this article adopts the second alternative on the grounds that it is difficult, if not impossible, to provide a unique experimental characterization of the weakly associated formal complex(es) that might be invoked to account for stabilization by solvent or by "inert" cosolutes. Cosolutes are distinguished operationally from nonwater constituents of the solvent by their absence from the reference system. 9 A. P. Minton, Biopolymers 20, 2093 (1981).

When the rate of encounter of A and B is very small relative to the rate with which (AB)* is converted to C, then the reaction is said to be diffusion limited. Under such conditions the rate of reaction is equal to the encounter rate kencCACB, where kent is proportional to the sum of the probe diffusion coefficients of A and B. These generally decrease approximately exponentially with increasing concentrations of volumeexcluding cosolutes, leading to the approximate relation 9 kf = kenc ~ k~nc exp(-gCve)

where k ~ncis the encounter rate constant in the absence of volume-excluding background species, Cve is the concentration of the predominant volumeexcluding cosolute, and g is a function of the relative sizes and shapes of A, B, and the predominant volume-excluding cosolute. Intermediate case. In the absence of large concentrations of space-filling cosolutes, most reactions are transition-state limited. Addition of spacefilling cosolutes tends to increase F (see later) and would therefore be expected to increase the overall rate of formation of C. However, the encounter rate is expected to decrease with an increasing concentration of space-filling cosolutes and at some point must become rate limiting, leading to the following simple approximation for the overall rate constant1°:

Hard Particle Models Although the potential of average force acting between actual solute molecules in solution must be considerably m o r e complex, it has been $ The potential of average force acting between n solute molecules in a particular configuration (relative positions, orientations) is actually the equilibrium free energy of a solution containing water, cosolvents (e.g., salts, buffers), and the n solute molecules in the fixed configuration, relative to the equilibrium free energy of the same solution when the n solute molecules are separated infinitely. u W. G. McMillan, Jr. and J. E. Mayer, J. Chem. Phys. 13, 276 (1945). 12j. O. Hirschfelder, C. F. Curtiss, and R. B. Bird, "Molecular Theory of Gases and Liquids." Wiley, New York, 1964.

[Page 7]

found (see later) that the classical colligative properties of solutions of globular proteins can be well accounted for over a very wide range of concentrations by a model in which the potential of average force is represented by a simple hard sphere potential: U(rq) = {O

rq<-ri+rJ rij > ri + r/

This finding led to the hard particle model for solutions of rigid macrosolutes, 13 according to which each molecule of macrosolute is represented by an equivalent hard particle, the dimensions of which (subject to constraints discussed later) resemble those of the actual solute molecule.

Evaluation of Two-Body Interaction Coefficients in the Context of the Hard Particle Model The concept of excluded volume becomes manifest in the context of a hard particle model. From Eqs. (12) and (14), the two-body interaction coefficient for two molecular species represented by hard spheres of radii rl and r2 is calculated to be Na4rr(rl + r2)3/3, which is precisely the volume excluded to the center of the mass of species 2 by a mole of species 1 or the volume excluded to the center of mass of species 1 by a mole of species 2. Not all macrosolutes may be approximately modeled by hard spheres. More generally, a rigid solute species is represented by a simple convex body (such as a sphere, ellipsoid of rotation, or spherocylinder) having dimensions similar to those of a three-dimensional model of the atomic structure viewed at low resolution. The potential of average force acting between the molecules in solution is assumed to be zero (or nearly zero) at all center-to-center distances exceeding the contact distance for a given relative orientation and infinite at distances less than or equal to the contact distance. As in the case of hard spheres, the two-body interaction coefficient is given by

Bij = Xa Vq

where Vq denotes the volume excluded to the center of mass of one particle of species i by a particle of species j (or vice versa), averaged over all mutual orientations of the two particles. Vq is often referred to as the

T w o - B o d y Interaction Coefficient for Interacting Hard Spheres

Under conditions such that the macrosolutes bear large net charges or when salt concentrations are very low, biological macromolecules may interact via nonspecific electrostatic repulsion or attraction. Two-body interaction coefficients have been calculated for particles interacting via simple angle-independent model potentials (or potentials of average force in the present context). 12 The simplest of these is the square well potential:

where BqHS is the two-body interaction coefficient for hard spheres and x ~ A/(ri + rj). The salient point of this result is that Bq may be greater than or less than B qHS, depending on the sign of W, and may even become equal to or less than zero for physically reasonable values of W and x. A sufficiently attractive potential can therefore cancel the effect of volume exclusion, at least at the level of the two-body interaction coefficient.I6 The three-body interaction coefficient has also been evaluated14; the resulting expression is quite complex. 16 A. P. Minton, Biophys. Chem. 57, 65 (1995).

[Page 10]

T w o - B o d y Interaction Coefficient f o r Hard-Sphere and R a n d o m Coil

The addition of "inert" water-soluble polymers has been reported to significantly influence a wide variety of biochemical reaction rates and equilibria. Although it is doubtful that all of the observed effects are due exclusively to differential volume exclusion of species of the reaction unit by polymer, it is likely that differential excluded volume provides much of the alteration in standard state free energy changes underlying the observed phenomena. However, a general quantitative treatment of volume exclusion between a compact rigid particle modeled as a hard sphere and a polymer modeled as a "hard" random coil has proved theoretically challenging. Historically, the problem seemed tractable only in two limiting cases. When the hard sphere is considered small relative to the polymer chain (i.e., has a radius that is of the order of the thickness of the coil or smaller), a hard sphere-hard rod approximation was used to calculate the two-body interaction coefficient. At the other extreme, when the hard sphere is considered large relative to the polymer chain (i.e., has a diameter that is of the order of the Stokes radius of the polymer chain or larger), a hard sphere-hard sphere approximation was used to calculate two-body and higher-order interaction coefficients.17'18An early attempt to treat the random coil explicitly19 had some success in accounting for the dependence of the chemical potential of bovine serum albumin on the concentration of added polyethylene glycol.2° More recently, the following expression for the covolume of a hard sphere of radius r, and a random coil polymer characterized as an effective Brownian walk with root mean square endto-end length r,. was derived21: Vsc =

Closed Equations for Hard Particle Models Although certain excluded volume phenomena, such as the reduction in protein solubility observed on addition of "inert" polymer, seem to be dominated by two-body interactions, a quantitative description of other phenomena, such as the concentration dependence of the colligative properties of concentrated protein solutions, requires several higher-order terms in Eq. (13). Although the expansion of solute activity in powers of solute concentration has a rigorous statistical-thermodynamic basis, evaluation of higher-order interaction coefficients is prohibitively difficult for models more complex than the single-species hard sphere fluid. Thus expansion of solute chemical potential in powers of solute concentration is not a generally practical approach to the quantitative analysis or prediction of excluded volume effects in solutions containing two or more macromolecular solute species at high concentration.

Closed Equation for the Hard-Sphere Model For an ensemble of identical hard spherical particles, interaction coefficients up to By have been evaluated analytically and via numerical integration23; these are presented in Table III. It is observed that to within 1% (i.e., the precision of the Monte Carlo numerical integration method used to evaluate the higher-order interaction coefficients) B6 and B7 may be calculated from B5 using the empirical relation Bn = 0.171B2B,, 1

suggesting that higher-order hard-sphere interaction coefficients may be reasonably reliably estimated using this empirical extrapolation formula. 23 F. H. Ree and W. G. Hoover, J. Chem. Phys. 46, 4181 (1967).

Equation (21) predicts that the chemical potential of a sphere in a hardsphere fluid should diverge when the denominator of the last term vanishes, i.e., when 0.171B2c ~ 1. Because B2c is equal to 8~b,where ~bis the fraction of total volume occupied by spheres, for identical hard spheres, (21) thus predicts that ~bcannot exceed a value of 1/(0.171 × 8) = 0.73, in excellent agreement with the fraction of volume occupied by identical hard spheres at hexagonal close packing (0.74).

Closed Equations Derived from Theories of Hard Particle Fluids A number of approximate equations of state for hard-sphere fluids have been reviewed by Boublfk.24 The usefulness of such approximate equations of state is commonly assessed on the basis of comparison between predicted properties of a hard particle fluid (such as the pressure or radial distribution function) on one hand, and from molecular dynamics or Monte Carlo computer simulations of the fluid on the other hand. Some equations of state are primarily empirical, whereas others are based on theoretical treatments that are exact in certain size or concentration limits. Each of these equations of state provides a closed expression for the chemical potential of a particular species of particle in such a fluid as a function of the sizes, shapes, and number densities of all particle species present in the fluid. The most widely studied and broadly applied of the hard particle theories is the scaled particle theory (SPT), 15'25-28 which has been found to account for the concentration dependence of the osmotic pressure of hemoglobin up to a concentration of ca. 400

where ((X)) ~- XpkXk and ((1)) ------Xpk. The activity coefficient of a probe species, represented by a single sphere in a fluid of "background" hard spheres, calculated using Eq. (22), is plotted in Fig. 2 as a function of the density of background spheres and as a function of the size of the probe sphere relative to the size of the background spheres. The results presented illustrate two important effects that are independent of details of particle shape: (1) For a given density of background particles, the magnitude of the increase in chemical potential of the probe depends strongly on the size of the probe relative to the size of the background species and in general becomes qualitatively important when the probe is about as large or larger than the primary background species. (2) Depending on the relative sizes of background and probe particles, excluded volume may result in substantial increases in probe chemical potential at relatively modest concentrations of background species.

The hard-sphere model Eqs. (24)-(26) describe well the concentration dependence of osmotic pressure, sedimentation equilibrium, and light scattering data obtained in hemoglobin and serum albumin solutions over a broad range of concentrations. 13'16'29'32-3n A few examples (taken from a large selection) are presented in Figs. 3, 4, and 5. Under experimental conditions, such that long-range electrostatic forces between protein molecules are damped out (i.e., moderate ionic strength, pH near the isoelectric point), the radius of the effective hard sphere that best accounts for the concentration dependence of the observed colligative property is found to be close to the average radius of the protein molecule viewed at low resolution. In the absence of long-range electrostatic interactions, integration over all mutual orientations of two interacting molecules tends to cancel out the competing effects of short-range attractions and repulsions associated with specific mutual orientations, leaving core steric repulsion as the major determinant of the two-body and higher interaction coefficients. However, under conditions such that electrostatic forces are expected to contribute significantly to the interparticle potential of average force, the radius of the effective hard sphere best fitting the data may be significantly larger or smaller than that of the actual molecule, depending on whether the net electrostatic interaction is repulsive or attractive (compare the previous discussion of square well potential). As emphasized before, the accuracy of any quantitative description of nonideal behavior based on a hard particle model depends critically on (1) the extent to which the actual potential of average force acting between 32 p. macrosolutes in an aqueous solution resembles a particular model hard particle potential and (2) the extent to which the particular theory employed (e.g., SPT) provides an accurate description of the actual properties of a hard particle fluid.~ Because both of these conditions are satisfied only approximately at best, one should not expect any quantitative relation based on a hard particle model/theory to provide more than a reasonable approximation to the behavior of any real solution.** Keeping in mind the limitations of the hard particle model, the extent to which a variety of experimental data obtained over a large range of concentrations can be rationalized semiquantitatively with the aid of simple hard particle models is truly impressive and provides ample justification ][ The modeling of an aqueous solution by a fluid of hard particles representing macrosolutes has been criticized on the grounds that neglect of solvent (water) as an explicit species of particle leads to a nonphysical result, namely that the activity of a test solute increases monotonically as the size of an "inert" cosolute decreases toward the size of a water moleculeY The nonphysical result is due not to the neglect of water as an explicit species, but rather to failure of the assumption that the potential of average force acting between small cosolutes and other solute species in water resembles a hard particle potential. ** In view of these limitations, it is the author's view that relatively small discrepancies between the predictions of a simplified hard particle model and any particular experimental measurement do not provide a firm basis on which to construct mechanistic theories of solute-solvent interaction. 29

H a r d Particle Model Calculations of Effect of Volume Exclusion on Reaction Equilibria The use of hard particle models to estimate the magnitude of excluded volume effects on a variety of reaction equilibria has been described in numerous publications cited earlier. A single simple example is presented here. Consider a protein (A) that may form an equilibrium dimer. 2A1 ~ A2 We wish to calculate the effect of addition of an arbitrary amount of a second "inert" protein (B) to a dilute solution of A on the equilibrium constant for dimerization of A. I n t e r p r e t a t i o n of the Effect of C o s o l u t e s on Reaction Equilibria: Differential H y d r a t i o n V e r s u s Differential V o l u m e Exclusion A number of papers, most of them cited in a recent review, 36 have been published in which the perturbation of a reaction equilibrium associated with the addition of an "inert" cosolute is attributed to the difference in "hydration" between the reactant(s) and the product(s). According to the analysis described in this article, such effects are attributed to the difference in volume excluded to cosolute by the reactant(s) and the product(s). The solvent is not treated explicitly, although it is taken into account implicitly as a determinant of the potential of average force acting between solute molecules. Can these two interpretations be reconciled?

seen to be identical in the limit of low cosolute concentration, except that the identification by Parsegian et al. 36 of AV2e~was the differential volume "of water" that is inaccessible to cosolute is found to be insufficiently general; AVZ~wis simply the differential volume that is inaccessible to cosolute. Differences between the excluded volume and the hydration analyses become evident at higher cosolute concentrations. Equation (35) predicts a linear relation between In K and 17 for a single species of cosolute that is ordinarily not observed experimentally. 36'3v In order to accommodate the observed nonlinearity, it has been suggested that the absolute degree of hydration of the two conformers of the macrosolute is influenced by the concentration of cosolute. 37 Such an ad h o c rationalization is inconsistent with the original two-state formulation of the model and is not susceptible to independent test. In contrast, Eqs. (31) and (32) indicate clearly that a relationship of precisely the form observed experimentally (i.e., a concave downward plot of log K against 17) is a trivial consequence of the experimentally observed concave upward dependence of II on c3.38 Finally, analysis of the phenomenon in terms of changes in the covolume of hydrated macrosolute and cosolute would seem to be more amenable to a structural interpretation than analysis in terms of changes in the amount of a phenomenologically defined "cosolute-inaccessible water."
